# Satvik Jain -- Complete Career Profile

This document contains everything known about Satvik Jain's career and life, written in full paragraphs with no bullet points, organized chronologically. Every sentence is expanded with context so that no meaning is lost. Where I am making an assumption, I have flagged it explicitly. The final section lists gaps and questions Satvik can answer to fill them.

---

## 1. Early Life, Family, and Tragedy (1999--2010)

Satvik Jain was born and grew up in a family that moved frequently across Madhya Pradesh and later Jharkhand due to his father's career as a bank manager at Canara Bank. His father was deeply dedicated to the family and invested heavily in Satvik's holistic development from an early age. He enrolled Satvik in UCMAS Abacus Mental Math classes at a very young age, drawing and sketching classes, classical music vocal training, and various other activities. His father would wake up at 4 AM every morning to carry Satvik and his younger sister Sanika's school bags to the school van pickup point by 5:30 AM. He took the family on trips to Kullu Manali, Shimla, Gangtok, and Assam, and arranged for the children to travel by airplane for the first time.

On July 8th, 2010, when Satvik was just 11 years old, his father took his own life. The family was on summer vacation at Satvik's nani's house at the time. The circumstances remain painful and uncertain -- the family suspects someone may have been pressurizing him, possibly related to a wrong judgment or bribe during his tenure as a bank manager. Someone reportedly showed up at the bank branch where he was visiting on the morning he died, and whatever exchange occurred may have pushed him to take that extreme step. The family has never fully confirmed whether it was a voluntary decision or a forced one.

This loss was devastating. Satvik was suddenly thrust into the role of the eldest and only male in the family, responsible for his younger sister Sanika and his mother. There was no other family support structure. He cried frequently in the beginning but gradually realized he needed to be the strong one. He had to grow up ten years overnight. This tragedy became the defining moment that shaped his resilience, his determination, and his deep understanding of mental health struggles. For the next 15 years, he rarely allowed himself to remember his father deeply because he knew it would make him cry, and he felt he had to remain strong for the family. He essentially erased many images of his father from his mind as a coping mechanism.

---

## 2. Schooling and Early Achievements in Chhatarpur (2010--2017)

After his father's death, the family spent approximately one year at Satvik's nani's (maternal grandmother's) house in Panna before moving to Chhatarpur, which became and remains the family's home base -- his mother still lives there today. After the move, Satvik's academic focus intensified as a way to honor his father's memory and secure the family's future. Despite the constant relocations during his father's lifetime and the trauma of his loss, Satvik maintained his position as a topper throughout his schooling.

In approximately 2013-2014, when Satvik was in Class 9th, he secured Rank 3 at the State level in the 9th State Level UCMAS Abacus Mental Math Competition. This qualified him for the National championship round. UCMAS (Universal Concept of Mental Arithmetic System) is a program that trains children in mental calculation using the abacus method, and the training had been started by his father years earlier. Reaching the state level podium in this competition demonstrates the early mathematical aptitude that his father had nurtured.

Satvik was also selected as one of the Top 2 students out of 125 CBSE Board students in all of Madhya Pradesh for the national Vigyan Manthan Yatra science exploration trip. This is a government-organized national science trip that selects a very small number of students from each state based on academic merit and scientific aptitude. Being chosen as one of just two students from the entire CBSE system in Madhya Pradesh signals that he was among the strongest science students in the state.

In his Class 10th CBSE Board Exams in 2015, Satvik secured Rank 1 in his school with a perfect 10/10 CGPA. As a result, he was felicitated by Smriti Irani, who was the Education Minister of India at that time (not a state-level minister). Being recognized personally by the Union Education Minister of India is a national-level distinction, far beyond a school or district honor.

For his Class 12th education, Satvik attended S.S.M Sr. Secondary School in Chhatarpur, Madhya Pradesh, and took the MPBSE (Madhya Pradesh Board of Secondary Education) 2017 examination. He secured Rank 1 in the entire Chhatarpur District with 94.2% marks, topping all schools in the district. Within his board exams, he scored a perfect 100/100 in both Mathematics and Chemistry, which earned him a merit scholarship worth Rs. 5,000. If he had scored just 4 more marks, he would have made it to Rank 10 in the state merit list as well, meaning he was on the very edge of being among the top 10 students in all of Madhya Pradesh.

What makes this achievement even more remarkable is context: Satvik had only prepared for his board exams for approximately 1 month. For the rest of the year, he had been in Kota doing intensive JEE coaching to prepare for the IIT entrance examination. So he achieved District Rank 1 and near-state-top-10 results with just one month of dedicated board exam preparation, while his primary focus was on one of the most competitive engineering entrance exams in the world.

---

## 3. JEE Preparation in Kota and the JEE Advanced Crisis (2015--2017)

Satvik moved to Kota -- the coaching capital of India -- for two years of intensive JEE preparation. The environment was intensely competitive, and he witnessed firsthand how the pressure affected many students, with numerous students facing depression and anxiety. This resonated deeply with him given his family's experience with his father's mental health struggles and death.

Satvik describes himself as a slow learner who needs time to build strong foundations before tackling complex problems. In Class 11th, he started slowly, but by Class 12th he had picked up tremendous pace because he had spent considerable time building his basics correctly. His strengths were in Physics and Mathematics -- he loved logical problem-solving and preferred practical applications of Newton's classical theories over theoretical quantum mechanics. He was always more interested in applying concepts than memorizing derivations and formulas. Chemistry was manageable but not a passion.

The day of JEE Advanced became one of the most defining moments of his life, not because of his performance, but because of a near-catastrophic mistake. During Paper 1, in the midst of intense concentration, he forgot to fill in the paper code -- a small administrative detail that was absolutely crucial for the paper to be evaluated. He only discovered this when filling in details for Paper 2, when an invigilator pointed it out.

For the next 3-4 minutes, he entered a deep, dark psychological state. His vision blurred, his hearing was compromised, there was literally darkness in his eyes, and his ears started ringing with a beeping sound. He felt like he was fainting. All the hard work of two years in Kota seemed to have gone in vain because of one administrative oversight in the biggest exam of his life.

But after those initial minutes of despair, something shifted. He asked himself: "What is the worst thing that could happen?" The answer was: nothing. He could prepare again and come back. He told himself: "I'm going to do what I came here for. I came to give this examination to the best of my ability, and that's exactly what I'm going to do today." He decided he would check his answers against answer keys, score himself, and if needed, prepare even harder and aim for AIR 1 the next year.

With this conviction, he continued writing. He scored 111 marks in Paper 1 and 127 marks in Paper 2. However, he lost approximately 16 marks due to the psychological disorientation -- filling wrong bubbles, making errors he would never make under normal conditions. His final ranks were JEE Mains: 9713 and JEE Advanced: 2446 (Top 0.002% of over 12 lakh candidates). The dramatic improvement from Mains to Advanced (9713 to 2446) reflected his strength in deep conceptual problem-solving that JEE Advanced demands, versus the speed-focused JEE Mains format.

---

## 4. IIT Delhi: Civil Engineering and Struggle (2017--2021)

Based on his JEE Advanced rank of 2446, Satvik secured admission to IIT Delhi. Civil Engineering was not his preferred branch -- it was simply what his rank could get him at the best possible college. His relatives convinced him that the IIT Delhi environment would be more valuable than the specific branch. Satvik chose college over branch, and he does not consider it a great decision in retrospect, because for the next four years he struggled significantly and did not enjoy the academic experience.

IIT Delhi's Civil Engineering curriculum was heavily theoretical, focused on rote learning and memorization -- the exact opposite of Satvik's learning style. He had always excelled at understanding concepts deeply and applying them practically, but the subjects required extensive memorization of empirical formulas and theoretical derivations without much practical application. He describes those four years as something he would like to forget. They were not a good time in his life.

His academic performance reflected this mismatch. He graduated with a CGPA of 6.43 out of 10. The maximum grade point he achieved in any theoretical subject was 8/10, and he never scored a 9. His first semester was particularly devastating -- he scored a 5.3 or 5.4 GPA, setting him back in a way that was nearly impossible to recover from over the remaining semesters.

The one area where Satvik consistently excelled was practical labs. He scored a 10/10 GPA in almost all of his lab and practical courses. He became the go-to person for his batch whenever they needed help with practical experiments, lab reports, presentations, or any hands-on work. His classmates recognized his strength in communication and practical application. This stark contrast -- failing in theoretical exams while excelling in labs -- reinforced his identity as a fundamentally practical, hands-on learner.

His B.Tech project was on the use of geothermal pile foundation for direct heating purposes. By his own admission, he did not do a great job on this project, but it was required for his coursework.

Satvik never sat for any campus placements. He never sat for any internships through the formal IIT placement system either. His only internship was a local one with a civil engineer in his home community who had taken the construction contract for a B.Ed. Government College. Satvik worked with him and observed how construction happens, but describes the learnings as minimal -- he primarily got a certificate. He had no other extracurricular activities at IIT beyond music and art, no technical projects, and no hackathons.

He was a resident of Udaigiri Hostel. During his time at IIT Delhi, his existing interest in Indian Classical Music continued. He had already been training for years (started by his father in childhood) and attained a Junior Diploma for Indian Classical Music Vocals, completing 5 years of vocal training in total. He also learned to play the Harmonium. He participated in the group song competition organized by the Mridang music society (IIT Delhi's music club) and secured Rank 2 amongst all 12 hostels as part of the Udaigiri hostel music team.

In visual art, he secured Rank 2 for Udaigiri Hostel in the Wall painting competition organized by the Fine Arts and Crafts Club at IIT Delhi, competing amongst all 12 hostels. At the community level in his hometown, he secured Rank 1 three times in consecutive Navratri celebrations for drawing and sketching competitions.

---

## 5. Depression and Recovery at IIT Delhi

Satvik's struggles at IIT Delhi were not just academic. In his first year, after the devastating 5.3-5.4 GPA in first semester, he went through a deeply painful personal experience. He had a crush on a girl who lived in his immediate neighborhood back home. They had been in contact, but she suddenly ghosted him completely without explanation -- one day they were talking, the next day she vanished, blocking all communication. This left a deep scar on his mental health.

He spiraled into depression that lasted for two to three semesters. He stopped trusting people entirely. He changed his wing in the hostel, moving away from his civil engineering batchmates, and found a new roommate on a different floor. He pushed himself into complete isolation, avoiding conversations and refusing to seek help. He once told his mother: "People have friend circles, but I don't even have a straight line. I don't even have two endpoints where I have a friend at the other end."

He dropped all hobbies, avoided social activities, and essentially became a ghost of his former self. He had stopped asking for help -- which he describes as probably his biggest mistake during that period. Pride, shame, and the belief that he had to handle everything alone prevented him from reaching out.

The turning point came through two relationships. First, his roommate Samarth, a mechanical engineering student who had successfully changed his branch through hard work and was excelling in coding. Samarth was hardworking, motivated, and consistently encouraged Satvik to keep working on himself. He was the only person Satvik trusted during those difficult times. Second, Satvik met his current girlfriend during an internship -- the person he plans to marry in 2027. After years of trust issues, this relationship slowly helped him break down barriers and learn to be vulnerable again. She helped him get out of the insecurity and restored his faith in human connections. They have been together for six years, and both families know about the relationship.

---

## 6. The Gap Year: GATE, ESE, and the PwC Decision (2021--2022)

After graduating from IIT Delhi in 2021, Satvik did not immediately join the workforce. He spent approximately 10 months preparing for the Engineering Services Examination (ESE) and the GATE examination. His goal was to secure a PSU (Public Sector Undertaking) job, reasoning that a government position would provide financial stability. He never considered the ESE seriously because he knew his weaknesses in memorization and theoretical recall. The primary focus was GATE.

This was also driven by his family situation. As the eldest and only male in the family -- with just his mother and sister Sanika -- he carried the responsibility of earning for and providing for the family. Not having a job by that point was a challenging and stressful situation.

During this preparation period, PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) offered him a full-time position in real estate dispute consulting -- a role that specifically sought civil engineers. He successfully cleared their interview process. However, PwC was offering 6-7 LPA, and Satvik had already set his mind on GATE preparation. He made the bold decision to decline PwC's offer and continue preparing, betting on himself to clear GATE and secure a PSU position paying 15-16 LPA -- more than double PwC's offer. This meant sacrificing a stable career at a prestigious firm to pursue an uncertain but potentially better outcome. This was a calculated risk made more difficult by the fact that he was an IIT graduate with a 6.43 CGPA, no work experience, no internships, and no guarantee of another job offer.

He took the GATE 2022 exam (likely February 2022) in the Civil Engineering stream. He scored 65 out of 100 marks (68 raw, reduced to 65 after normalization), securing an All India Rank of approximately 1463, placing him in the Top 1.5% of over 100,000 candidates. However, this rank was frustratingly close but not sufficient for PSU cutoffs. If he had scored just 6-7 more marks (around 71-72 after normalization), he would have easily made PSU interview cutoffs, and he was confident in his interview abilities.

With the PSU path closed and no interest in pursuing an M.Tech in Civil Engineering, Satvik had to pivot. He wanted to go into teaching -- it had been a profession he always aspired to, inspired by his love for mentoring. But the COVID lockdown had closed coaching institutes and they were not hiring faculty. He applied broadly -- faculty roles at coaching institutes (Rs. 40,000/month), teaching positions for 9th and 10th grade students, a project manager role at another company, and eventually found a work-from-home opportunity at Sprinklr.

---

## 7. Sprinklr: Product Implementation Consultant (April 2022 -- April 2023)

Satvik joined Sprinklr in April 2022 as a Product Implementation Consultant. When Sprinklr called for the interview, he wasn't entirely sure what the role entailed, but the company's work in social media analytics seemed interesting. The interview process was rigorous -- six rounds that tested various aspects of his capabilities. What became clear during these interviews was that his communication skills and hunger for learning were his strongest assets. For the first time since his college struggles, he realized that communication was actually his strength. The depression and isolation had made him forget this fundamental ability, but the interview process reminded him of what he was capable of.

As an Implementation Consultant in the insights department, Satvik was responsible for gathering data from social media platforms and creating dashboards and insights that helped businesses make decisions. This was his first exposure to data-driven decision making at scale.

His performance in this first year was exceptional. He secured Rank 1 and the Gold Medal Globally amongst all 28 Implementation Consultants across Sprinklr worldwide. This was not a regional ranking but a global one. As a direct result, he received a fast-tracked promotion to Senior Implementation Consultant within just 11 months.

During this period, he worked with several major enterprise clients. For Adobe, he reduced Time To Value by 75% by unifying unstructured social media data and automating data analysis using SQL and Data Pipelines. He managed the entire data pipeline lifecycle -- extraction, cleaning, transformation, and unification of Earned, Organic, and Paid data using SQL and Python. He also built and deployed a custom AI Model for Spam Detection for Adobe that achieved an F1 Score of 87%, filtering out spam content so Adobe's analytics were based on clean, relevant data. The spam detection work was part of the Adobe engagement, not a separate project.

For Snapchat, he increased month-over-month brand engagement by 15% in the music, sport, and fashion segments by analyzing user behavior and recommending engagement strategies.

For Discord, he reduced brand reputation risk by 25% by building a custom AI-based detection system for identifying hate comments on Discord servers.

For Mars Incorporation, he recommended product strategies by identifying trends across the Top 10 high-performing and low-performing products in their portfolio.

He improved Case Response Rate by 10% across customer care cases by creating AI-powered automated workflows for skill-based routing.

---

## 8. Sprinklr: Senior Product Implementation Consultant (April 2023 -- May 2024)

After his fast-tracked promotion, Satvik took on significantly more responsibility including larger client engagements, team leadership, and direct involvement with government-scale projects. The chronological order of major projects during this period and the subsequent Senior Product Analyst period is important: the Qatar CGB project came first, then Walmart, then Use Case Hub, and then the promotion to Senior Product Analyst.

### The Qatar CGB Project: Nation-Scale Digital Transformation

The first and largest project after Satvik's promotion to Senior IC was the Government of Qatar engagement -- one of the biggest projects in Sprinklr's implementation history. This was not a typical client deployment. Satvik was part of a landmark, nation-scale digital transformation initiative in Qatar -- the first country-wide Citizen Experience Management (CXM) program of its kind anywhere in the world.

The engagement was a $32 million, 5-year strategic partnership with the Government of Qatar (referred to internally as the CGB project). The objective was to implement a unified, AI-powered platform across more than 40 ministries and public entities, replacing fragmented legacy systems with a single solution to capture citizen feedback, generate insights, and enable real-time improvements in public services. This was more than a technology rollout -- it was a fundamental shift in how an entire government engages with its population. The program was aligned with Qatar National Vision 2030, focused on improving service quality, transparency, and digital maturity at a national level.

At the time the project began, Qatar ranked 78th globally in the UN E-Government Development Index. Following this digital transformation, the country advanced to rank 53 -- a jump of 25 positions -- reflecting tangible progress in digital governance and citizen experience. Satvik's work contributed to establishing a unified, data-driven view of citizen experience across all government entities, driving operational efficiency by consolidating multiple tools into one platform, and strengthening Qatar's global competitiveness and attractiveness for investment and talent. This project stands as a flagship example of how large-scale, cross-government CX transformation can deliver measurable national impact.

Within this massive engagement, Satvik built a personalized iOS mobile app called Sharek ("The Unified Citizen Experience Management Tool") for the Qatari government. Sharek enabled the Prime Minister's office to monitor citizen complaints across all 40 ministries using NLP sentiment analysis. The system delivered insights into governance issues within just 4 weeks from project initiation. He also managed the development of 21 Display Screens installed in government offices for real-time monitoring dashboards.

A particularly challenging aspect was Arabic localization, which required enforcing full right-to-left (RTL) UI adaptation throughout the entire interface. RTL localization is notoriously complex -- it requires rethinking layout, navigation flow, icon direction, and data visualization, not just translating text. He led a team of 5 members specifically for this localization effort. He designed a smart alerts system for negative sentiment tracking, crisis monitoring, and executive monitoring. The measurable app-level impact: 7-day retention improved from 40% to 55%, and time-to-insight was reduced from 2 days to 2 hours. He improved the F1 Score of the Sentiment model by 11% and the Intuition model by 13% for Arabic language validation. Communication with government stakeholders was handled through weekly asynchronous visual updates.

### Team Scaling During CGB

The team scaling from 6 to 14 consultants happened during the CGB project itself. As the Qatar engagement grew in scope, Satvik needed to rapidly expand his team to handle the workload. He scaled from 6 to 14 members in 3 months using a structured "reverse-KT" (Knowledge Transfer) onboarding process -- instead of the traditional approach where existing team members sit down and teach new joiners, the process was structured so that new joiners actively extracted knowledge through a systematic framework. He then led a team of 8 of these consultants to deliver over 120 Personalized Dashboards for the 40 government ministries within 4 weeks. Each dashboard was customized to the specific needs and data of the individual ministry.

For Rolex, he improved the product usability of Sprinklr's CXM platform on a $2 Million account, leading the development of 11+ product enhancement requests. This was product-level work, iver and above his Senior IC responsibilities where he worked with the product and data science teams to create customised ML models.

Across his broader portfolio during this period, he improved customer happiness to an average score of 9.14/10 across 36 projects in 15+ industry verticals. He reduced churn rate from 13% to 9% by achieving a perfect 10/10 CHI (Customer Happiness Index) score on 66% of his projects generating 1.6 million dollars in direct consultant timecard revenue for Sprinklr. He mentored a team of 13 Consultants, helping them learn the 7 Insight Products through a real-life case-based approach to problem solving.

### Walmart Spark Driver Support: GenAI Root-Cause Analysis

After the CGB project, Satvik moved on to the Walmart Spark Driver Support engagement. The project had three goals: (1) the contact volume was extremely high at 100K+ contacts, and Walmart needed to understand what was driving it; (2) driver experience was extremely poor, with churn so severe that nearly 70% of drivers churned out even before completing their first delivery -- retention analysis across 4 monthly cohorts showed that D7 retention was reasonable at approximately 65%, but D30 retention dropped sharply to around 35%, and D60 retention collapsed to roughly 18%, indicating a massive falloff between the first week and the first two months; and (3) Walmart wanted to use the insights to improve their product roadmap by understanding the root causes of driver dissatisfaction.

Satvik built a GenAI root-cause product that analyzed the 100K+ contact center calls using unsupervised ML clustering and correlation detection to identify root causes, and auto-routed mitigation actions to engineering and fraud teams. The data sources included In-app reviews, Apple app store/Google play store reviews, nps surveys, voice call transcripts, customer support emails and customer support chat exports, and public social media reviews like Reddit, Twitter, Facebook etc in raw form for which he did data preprocessing using python scripts. He designed a custom L1/L2/L3 issue taxonomy (a three-level categorization from broad to specific) and built a unified data architecture joining internal support data with public social escalation data via SQL-based backend joins. The time-to-insight went from 7 days (the old manual analysis process) to same-day delivery.

A notable highlight of this project was the Black Friday pilot. During the Black Friday, Cyber Monday, and the surrounding weekend (a 3-4 day period), Satvik's team ran a live pilot where they were constantly monitoring and sharing real-time insights to the Walmart team. They maintained a dedicated WhatsApp group with the Walmart stakeholders and were in continuous communication to validate whether the AI-generated insights were accurate and actionable. This pilot demonstrated the system's ability to operate in real-time under extreme volume conditions.

The VP of Product, Anish Singal, was deeply involved in the Walmart project. This was the first time he saw Satvik's groundwork, ownership, and ability to drive a project firsthand. This visibility fundamentally changed Satvik's image in front of leadership and set the stage for his eventual transition into product management -- though not before the Use Case Hub work.

The revenue impact of the Walmart project was $1.2M TCV from POC-to-deal conversion, and the broader Root Cause Analysis MVP resulted in a $9M+ pipeline opportunity for Q4 FY24. After Satvik left Sprinklr, Walmart signed a $2 million deal for a follow-on project called Last Mile Delivery, representing the continued value of the foundation Satvik built. Satvik created a strong reputation with the Solution Consulting leadership through this project.

### Use Case Hub: Transition to Product Management

After Walmart, Satvik began working on the Use Case Hub -- and this is where the transition from implementation to product management effectively happened, even before the formal title change. Critically, he was doing both roles simultaneously during this period. He was still handling his Senior IC responsibilities in the US time zone while working with the product team in the India time zone. His workdays stretched from 9 AM to 3 AM -- an 18-hour daily grind with no additional compensation, no overtime pay, and no merit increase. This dual-role period was exhausting but strategically important.

The Use Case Hub was the flagship product achievement. Satvik grew adoption from 35% to 85% across Sprinklr's 1,500+ SaaS clients. The Use Case Hub enabled self-serve setup for 15 industry verticals, eliminating 7-day manual configuration and reducing Time To Value by 75%. He conceptualized the "Listen, Learn, and Act" framework from scratch, which became the product's core architecture. He analyzed 1,500+ existing client dashboards to extract industry best practices and built a template architecture from real usage data. He conducted usability and A/B tests, integrated the solution into the main dashboard creation journey, and enabled self-service for clients with less than $1M ARR.

The Use Case Hub included the AI Reporting automation capabilities: a 2-step user journey delivering dashboards in under 10 seconds and a 15% revenue increase in the first 3 months.

### Parallel Activities During Senior IC Period

During this entire period, even without an official product management title, Satvik was practicing product management in everything but name. His implementation consultant role reported to the VP of Product, giving him direct exposure to product thinking and processes. On the side, he was building startup decks, writing PRDs for his own ideas, participating in startup weekends, and essentially doing product management work in his personal time. The transition to wanting to become a product manager was gradual through Sprinklr -- it did not happen suddenly. It happened by working with users, seeing that they were facing pain points, and wanting to fix those issues himself. He was also pushing internally for a transition to a formal PM role, but encountered organizational challenges and favoritism that blocked the move.

He had applied to American Express during this period and reached final rounds, but was rejected with feedback that he lacked relevant PM work experience. Rather than being discouraged, he used this feedback as a roadmap -- he needed formal PM credentials to match the work he was already doing.

He enrolled in the Feynman Fellowship at Global Tech Initiative (a pre-MBA program led by former BCG consultant Naman Srivastava and Shatabshi Sharma), which gave him exposure to management consulting, product management, corporate policy, and startups. He won the startup weekend at this program and was a top performer. He also enrolled in Data Science and Machine Learning at Scaler Academy (starting October 2023), building formal structure around his SQL, Tableau, Excel, and Python skills.

---

## 9. Sprinklr: Senior Product Analyst, Advocacy Strategy, and Final Months (May 2024 -- July 2024)

After the Use Case Hub work and the visibility gained from the Walmart and CGB projects, Satvik was finally promoted to Senior Product Analyst in May 2024. On his PM-facing resumes, he compresses the entire Sprinklr tenure (April 2022 -- July 2024) under this single title. The underlying work spans three distinct roles with a clear growth trajectory from Implementation Consultant to Senior IC to Senior Product Analyst.

After the Use Case Hub, Satvik moved into the Advocacy team for his final one to two months at Sprinklr. The Advocacy product was an employee advocacy platform with 182 active customers, growing from $7M ARR in Q2 2024 to $9.8M ARR in Q3 2024, with 40% year-over-year active CARR growth. During this short stint, Satvik's role was purely strategic -- he developed the product strategy and roadmap for FY-25 but did not execute on any of it. He presented the strategy document to his VP (not at any external summit). The document covered five core business pillars (Brand Awareness, Reduced Marketing Costs, Lead Generation, Social Recruiting, and Employee Communication), a competitive landscape analysis against players like FirstUp, EveryoneSocial, Sprout Social, DSMN8, and Haiilo in a $400-700M market growing at 9-10% CAGR, and a quarterly feature roadmap spanning Q1 through Q4 FY24-25 with prioritized initiatives across Employee Advocacy, Integrations (MS Teams, WhatsApp, Adobe Analytics), Social Recruiting (Workday/LinkedIn Jobs), and Employee Communication. He identified Sprinklr's core defensible differentiators as enterprise-grade compliance and governance, a unified platform offering, branded user interface, 13-language support, and personal social media management capabilities. This was strategy and roadmap work only -- he left Sprinklr before any execution began.

By this point, Satvik was completely burned out from the constant struggle to get recognition and fair opportunities at Sprinklr. The company wasn't providing compensation that inspired him to stay, and he felt his growth was being artificially limited by politics and favoritism rather than merit. His title change to Senior Product Analyst happened just five days before his second American Express interview.

---

## 10. American Express: Senior Associate Product Manager (July 2024 -- Present)

Satvik joined American Express in July 2024 as a Senior Associate Product Manager. During the interview, his hiring manager candidly told him they generally looked for candidates with 2-3 years of experience and that he had very little. Satvik's response captured his career philosophy: "Experiencing things gives you experience, not the years of experience. It doesn't matter how many years you have worked in a company. If you have not experienced those things, then you will never be able to get experience."

He was placed on the AML (Anti-Money Laundering) Risk Scoring Engine team, working on the Customer Risk Rating (CRR) platform that powers risk scoring across 100M+ customer accounts in 40+ markets. He leads an 18-member scrum team including engineers, data engineers, QA, Product Owners, and a UX designer.

The team was understaffed when he joined, and even though his title was Senior APM, he was functioning as an end-to-end product manager. He led 10 sprints single-handedly without any manager or direct supervision.

What Satvik accomplished at Amex was done almost entirely by himself, without any manager supervision or direct involvement. His first assignment upon joining in July 2024 was the vendor evaluation for building the CRR platform in-house versus using third-party vendors. The evaluation ran from approximately July to September 2024. The vendors assessed included NICE Actimize (a Forrester and Gartner leader in AML solutions), Symphony AI (which offered the BAE Solutions CRR product), and SAS. Each vendor was evaluated against criteria including functional fit, configurability, integration capability, cost, and long-term scalability. Satvik conducted the analysis, prepared the recommendation, and secured sign-off from leadership on the in-house build decision. This was a critical strategic decision that determined the direction of the entire AML platform.

After securing the in-house build decision, he turned to the BRD -- which contained 21 requirements that needed to be translated into an actionable product strategy. He spent the subsequent months deeply understanding the product end-to-end, gaining deep domain knowledge in AML risk scoring, and breaking down the BRD into a coherent structure. Based on the 21 requirements, he defined 6 capabilities and constructed a comprehensive 3-year product roadmap around November to December 2024. From PI 25.1 through 25.5, the team executed on this roadmap and delivered MVP-1 by the end of 2025.

The Leadership in Action Award and 6000 Blue rewards he received were for the totality of what he accomplished on his own: understanding the product end-to-end without guidance, conducting the vendor evaluation and securing the in-house build sign-off, breaking down the BRD into 6 capabilities, constructing the 3-year roadmap, leading 10 sprints single-handedly, and delivering MVP-1 -- all without any manager or direct supervisor involved. The award was given by Nerses Kazarian, the SVP of Risk Management at American Express, which reflects the seniority of the recognition.

The CRR platform operates within the existing 5-dimension risk framework (Customer, Geography, Transaction, Product, Events) that was already established at American Express.

The real pain points in the CRR platform were around configuration difficulty and speed to market. The existing system was a static, rule-based model where making changes was extremely painful. When a market needed to customize risk scoring rules, the process involved manually copying existing blueprints, modifying them for the specific market or module, and deploying the changes. This manual process introduced frequent errors, created duplication across configurations, generated hard-to-track dependencies between rules, and often required workarounds for functionality that the platform simply did not support natively. The speed to market for any configuration change was approximately 10 days. Through his work on the platform, this was reduced to approximately 3 days -- a roughly 70% reduction in time to market.

The BRD contained 21 requirements numbered 12.1 through 12.21, covering: CRR Framework Structure, Change Requests, Customer Updates, Customer Level Scoring, Configurability, Centralized Lists, Audit Trail, Sandbox Simulation, Sandbox Analysis, Model Governance, Certification Process, AML Integration, Reporting and Dashboards, UI/UX, Customer CRR View, Rescoring, Data Considerations, Performance Reporting, AI/ML Guidelines, Notifications, and User Access. From these 21 requirements, Satvik defined 6 core capabilities: Scoring Engine Configuration (the drag-and-drop rule builder), Asset Manager (a centralized area for managing all lists used in rule configuration -- such as lists of prohibited countries or high-risk industries -- where a list can be configured in one central place and even if it is referenced by 10 different rules, it can be edited from that single location), Sandbox Environment (simulation and versioning for testing rule changes before production deployment), Reporting and Dashboards, Audit Trail, and Customer Scoring View. He organized these into 3 parallel workstreams for execution.

He drove more than 20 UX research sessions with compliance analysts and managers across 6 regions. He designed 3 new capability UIs end-to-end: Asset Manager, Advanced Rule Configurations, and Sandbox Versioning. His product reached the staging and QA environment but was never deployed to production end-users because the project was stopped and pivoted to a data strategy focus, building a consolidated data layer. The priority for the CRR platform MVP was dropped in favor of this strategic pivot.

The team operated under the SAFe framework with quarterly PI Planning cycles. There were 2 scrum teams: a Rule Configuration team of approximately 12 members and a Rule Execution team of approximately 6 members. Satvik delivered 4 consecutive Zero Spillover PIs (no planned work spilled into the next planning increment), and the team delivered over 60 features during his tenure. His guiding motto for the team was "Don't build Cadence 2.0" -- meaning they should avoid simply replicating the legacy system with a new interface and instead rethink the product from first principles.

He navigated 2 major strategy pivots (re-baselining events) during 2025. The first happened at the end of PI 25.2: leadership initially planned to modernize the product and release it over two years, but then pivoted to requiring delivery within one year (by PI 26.1) with only the features necessary to decommission the legacy Cadence platform -- the bare minimum required, not a full modernization. This meant Satvik had to reprioritize the entire backlog within a single week, fundamentally shifting the scope and sequencing of delivery. The second pivot was the slowdown and eventual stopping of the CRR platform build because CRR cannot function without clean, standardized data. When one of the supporting teams underperformed on the data layer, the decision was made to pivot to a data strategy -- fixing the Consolidated Data Layer (CDL) first before continuing with CRR execution. This became Satvik's current project.

He onboarded and ramped up 4 resources: 3 Product Owners (Tahcin, Suprana, and Sakshi) and 1 UX designer (Shivani, who reports directly to him). Shivani was hired through a 12-round competitive selection process.

In January 2025, Satvik participated in the American Express Growth Hack, a 2-day innovation event. He captained an 8-member team that built an AI Backlog Grooming bot -- a Slack-integrated tool that used AI combined with Rally (Amex's project management tool) to automatically write user stories and product features. The team ranked #21 out of 400+ teams, placing in the Top 6%. The concept was later productionized by GMNS (Global Merchant and Network Services) as QE Assist.

He also built Shipquick, an Agentic AI tool for product managers that generates professional, branded, single-file HTML documents from raw data -- strategy reports, dashboards, executive briefs, and presentations. Shipquick is part of the broader LinkRight product suite. He demoed it to VPs at American Express, and this demonstration led to him being given a stretch role on the Negative News and Adverse Media Triage Agent project -- integrating Work Fusion's EvantAgent into the financial crimes platform for automated screening of potentially risky customer profiles. He drove ChatGPT Enterprise Adoption within his team, achieving 80% AI-assisted story drafting for sprint planning and backlog management.

He won the Rangoli competition at American Express, leading a team of 15-18 people with a concept integrating Amex values and culture, delivering the winning speech. He also created an AI-generated song for EVP Ravi Verma as a creative gesture that gained visibility.

He gained 8,000-9,000 LinkedIn followers within 2-3 months through authentic personal branding content -- sharing his daily experiences, growth and learnings as a product manager, mentoring people, helping with job search and job hunt advice, and building a leadership voice.

### Consolidated Data Layer (CDL) Strategy -- Current Project (PI 26.1 -- Present)

Satvik's most recent and current project at American Express is the Consolidated Data Layer (CDL) strategy, which emerged from the second strategic pivot. The core problem is this: over the past year, a data platform team built a Data Access Manager (DAM) layer as a unified data source for GCIP (Global Compliance Intelligence Platform). While the DAM was an important foundational step, it delivers only fractionally standardized values -- a significant portion of data remains in raw, unstandardized SOR (System of Record) formats. The CRR product cannot use this data for accurate risk scoring because rules require consistently standardized and categorized values to function correctly. This data gap became the key dependency blocking CRR modernization.

To illustrate the scale: for Product Type alone, there are 136,738 raw unstandardized source records that need to be compressed to 12,408 standardized records and then to just 64 business-readable categories -- an 11:1 compression ratio from source to standardized, and a 2,137:1 compression from source to categories. For Industry Type, the numbers are even larger: 521,583 raw records compressing to 42,182 standardized and just 98 categories (12.3:1 and 5,322:1 compression ratios respectively). Currently, the cross-reference and categorization mappings are maintained fully manually by the Cadence business team via Excel uploads -- painful, error-prone, and unscalable.

Satvik built the CDL strategy and execution plan. The CDL defines a 5-step data pipeline: Translation (mapping raw SOR values to standard codes), Standardization (normalizing across sources), Categorization (grouping into business-readable categories), Derivation (computing derived data points from standardized inputs), and Master List (maintaining authoritative reference data). The scope covers 54 data points across Individual and Entity/Intermediary products. Of these, 8 require no standardization (they are already clean), 21 are in the P1+P2 priority group covering 58% of risk elements (67 out of 115), 17 are in P3+P4 covering 96.5% of risk elements (111 out of 115), and 8 are in a backlog group pending business confirmation. The strategy aligns with BRD requirements 12.16 (Rescoring Capabilities), 12.17 (Data Considerations -- specifically the data mapping resolution mandate), 12.18 (Performance Reporting), and 12.19 (AI/ML Based Scoring, which requires clean standardized training data as a prerequisite).

The execution roadmap spans PI 26.2 through 27.5, with Phase 0 (pre-standardization and discovery) in PI 26.2-26.3, Phase 1 (Individual portfolio -- P1 through P4 data points) in PI 26.3-27.1, Phase 2 (Entity/Intermediary portfolio) in PI 26.4-27.5, and continuous data governance running throughout. The strategy also includes an AI/ML POC for automated standardization and a Master List implementation for centralized, governed reference data. Satvik currently works on this project with a small team of 4 members with direct visibility to leadership.

## 11. GoGoGo Startup Involvement (September 2024 -- June 2025)

Starting around September 2024, while at American Express, Satvik became involved with GoGoGo, a ride-hailing startup founded by serial entrepreneur Avinash Kumar. The mission was to build a customer-centric, driver-focused cab aggregator platform powered by a subscription model, competing with Ola and Uber, with a long-term vision of expanding into home services, food delivery, and other services to uplift the gig driver community.

Avinash initially approached Satvik to be his technical co-founder. Satvik declined due to bandwidth constraints (he was still building his career at Amex) but committed as an investor and advisor. He provided financial support during a period when Avinash was essentially broke. His role evolved into something like a Chief of Staff or Founder's Office capacity. He helped build the team, met co-founders, investors, and advisors. Avinash's eventual co-founder, Gagan Sawhney (Sahni), brought credentials from McKinsey, Bain, Adobe, PayU, and Salesforce.

Through GoGoGo, Satvik gained firsthand exposure to fundraising, team building, product development cycles, regulatory compliance, and the emotional rollercoaster of entrepreneurship. He also discovered that GoGoGo was using Jungleworks' platform, whose founder Samar Singla had visited IIT Delhi during Satvik's time there and shared his story of building a startup despite a low CGPA. Satvik left GoGoGo by approximately June 5, 2025, though he remains involved through bi-weekly board calls where he interacts with advisors and directors.

GoGoGo is currently operational in Indore and is expanding to 11 more cities -- it has started in Vadodara and will also launch in Surat and several other cities. The total funding to date is approximately Rs. 2.5 crore, having raised its pre-seed round. The startup is experiencing losses as no venture capital is currently interested in the ride-hailing domain, but it is surviving with approximately 5 months of runway. The immediate priorities are cutting losses and accelerating growth to keep the startup running.

## 14. Masters Union Decision and LinkRight (2025)

In June 2025, Satvik applied to Masters Union and received an acceptance letter with a 50% scholarship. Masters Union's pedagogy of "learn business by doing business" aligned with his beliefs. However, even with the scholarship, the remaining fees would be 13-14 lakhs, plus the opportunity cost of giving up his 30 LPA salary at American Express for 16 months. His family and friends were overwhelmingly against it, viewing it as financially imprudent given his current salary level.

After deliberation, Satvik decided not to join Masters Union. His reason was that he wanted to build something real on the side rather than go back to an educational setting. He started solving for career navigation, and this is where the LinkRight journey began. What started as a resume optimization tool grew into a full AI career navigation suite with 4-5 products: Sync (resume signal engineering and JD optimization), Flex (LinkedIn content pipeline via "Knowledge Liquidity"), Scout (job portal monitoring), Shipquick (professional document builder -- the same tool demoed to VPs at Amex), and AutoFlow (n8n workflow automation). The vision is an agentic AI product suite that automates the entire lifecycle of landing a top-tier tech job -- turning raw life experience into structured career signals, tailored application artifacts, and strategic professional visibility. LinkRight has been in development since June 2025 as a passion project for Satvik. This is the same product referred to as "Resume ImproverAI" in his interests section on resumes.

They were expanding but faced significant operational challenges -- multiple broken manual processes, lack of technology integration, and siloed operations. ## 17. Mentorship Legacy

Mentorship is a deeply important part of Satvik's identity, rooted in his original aspiration of becoming a teacher.

His most significant mentorship story involves his sister Sanika. Having learned from his own mistake of choosing Civil Engineering based on others' advice, he guided her JEE preparation. She made it to IIT JEE with just one year of coaching preparation. He ensured she chose a coding-focused branch -- she picked Electronics and Communication Engineering at IIT BHU. Sanika coded almost 24 hours a day during college, became the only girl to secure a Google internship from IIT BHU, and topped the girls' category in Electronics with a 9.2/10 GPA. When she didn't get a pre-placement offer from Google, Satvik pushed her to apply for Confluent (package around Rs. 1 crore), telling her he wouldn't talk to her for the rest of his life if she didn't apply. She reached the final waitlist at Confluent (6th round), outcompeting CS graduates. She now works as Senior Software Developer at Oracle.

More recently, a junior colleague who was laid off from Sprinklr -- from a poor farming family in Rajasthan, educated at Jawahar Navodaya Vidyalaya and Super 30, graduated from NIT Warangal -- came to live with Satvik after the layoff. Over three months, Satvik provided intensive mentorship in product management, data analytics, interview skills, and professional communication. They gave 15-20 interviews together. The colleague secured an Associate Product Manager role at Next Wave without being jobless for a single day.

At Amex, Satvik onboarded and ramped up 3 Product Owners and 1 UX designer. At Sprinklr, he mentored 13 consultants on the 7 Insight Products.

His long-term vision is the "Jain Group of Companies" -- a diversified business empire inspired by Ratan Tata and the Ambani family, focused on technology-enabled solutions with strong social impact components, particularly around mental health. He envisions 40-50 years of business building.

## 20. Attentive.ai: Target Role and Positioning

Attentive.ai is a vertical SaaS startup providing AI-powered automated construction takeoff software (Beam AI) for the construction, landscape, and paving industries in North America. Series B stage ($48M raised, $30.5M led by Insight Partners), 1,200+ customers, 497 employees. Founded by IIT Delhi alumni Shiva Dhawan and Rishabjit Singh. The role is PM/SPM in Noida (in-office), owning segment P&L (ARR, churn, adoption, TAT) for a vertical like concrete subcontractors or utility suppliers.

Satvik's alignment is strong: ~3.8 years of product experience, demonstrated outcome ownership (not just features), full product lifecycle experience, data comfort, Figma wireframing, and proven mentorship. The unique differentiator is his B.Tech in Civil Engineering from IIT Delhi -- the same institution as the founders. He understands construction vocabulary and can empathize with the estimator persona. The narrative: "I studied how buildings are built. Now I want to build the software that helps the people who build them."

Key parallel: At Amex, compliance analysts relied on manual workflows they trusted for years. Adoption required earning trust through transparency, not mandate. The same dynamic exists with Beam AI -- estimators don't distrust the AI's accuracy, they distrust the change.

Gaps: No PostHog (mitigated by broader analytics stack), no vertical SaaS experience (mitigated by enterprise SaaS at Sprinklr across 15 verticals), no direct construction industry work experience (mitigated by Civil Engineering degree).

Attentive.ai is not the only opportunity Satvik is exploring. He is also looking at a Growth PM role at Navii (where he completed his short internship) and has applied to a PM role at Wishlink (currently in review stage, not yet shortlisted). The career strategy involves pursuing multiple options in parallel.

---

## 🏦 Story 1: CRR Build vs. Buy — CDD Platform Evaluation

> *Use this for: "Tell me about a time you led a complex analysis / influenced a major decision / worked with cross-functional teams"*

---

"So this was one of the more impactful pieces of work I did at Amex. We were modernizing our Customer Risk Rating system — CRR — and a key question in front of us was: do we buy an external CDD platform or build it in-house?

I led the entire evaluation end-to-end. We started by defining our requirements — what does an Enhanced CDD platform actually need to do for us? Things like configurable risk scoring, real-time capability, sandbox environments, feedback loops, and seamless integration with the Amex ecosystem.

From there, I ran a structured down-select process. We started with 15+ vendors in the market, applied criteria like regulatory reputation, scalability, security, integration capability — and narrowed it down to 8, then 3, and finally 2 finalists: NICE Actimize and Symphony AI.

Both went through a formal POC alongside our in-house build. And what was interesting is — Actimize was the Forrester/Gartner leader, a household name in financial crimes compliance. Symphony AI had better architecture, simpler APIs, and came with a product that was acquired from BAE Solutions, which was also a market leader. So on paper, both were strong.

But when we did the side-by-side comparison against our in-house POC — on customization, performance, cost, and integration fit with Amex's existing ecosystem — the in-house build had unique advantages that neither vendor could fully replicate. The vendors were expensive to integrate, had some out-of-box gaps in our specific use cases like hierarchy scoring, and there were collaboration challenges flagged as well.

So I built the recommendation and got stakeholder buy-in for the in-house build. The whole process ran from February to August 2024 — so about 6-7 months of structured evaluation. The key here was that I didn't just go with the vendor everyone assumed we'd pick. I made the case with data from the POC, and that's what drove the decision."

---

## 🌱 Story 2: Sukha Education Foundation — GGI Impact Lab

> *Use this for: "Tell me about a time outside your day job / leadership beyond work / passion projects or consulting experience"*

---

"Alongside my role at Amex, I did a program called the Global Governance Initiative — it's a certification in product management, public policy, and strategy consulting. And as part of that, we had an Impact Lab component where you actually apply your learning to a real-world problem with a real organization.

I was placed as a strategy consultant for Sukha Education Foundation — an NGO founded in 2021 that was training students through programs like Communicative English, a Book Club project, and a few other flagship initiatives. They had over 2,000 students trained and 25+ NGO and school partnerships. Great mission, great traction — but operationally, they were struggling to scale.

The core problem was fragmentation. They were running everything over WhatsApp groups — lesson plans being shared informally, attendance tracked in scattered spreadsheets, volunteers working in silos with no real collaboration structure. And because of that, volunteer churn was around 40%, which was a real cost — both in time and money.

My role was to design a digital transformation roadmap for them. I approached it like a proper strategy engagement. First, I mapped the current-state challenges across three buckets — communication, volunteer engagement, and operational complexity. Then I built the case for change using a cost-of-inaction analysis — basically showing that if they didn't act, they were looking at nearly ₹1.8 lakhs in avoidable costs over three years just from volunteer churn, admin inefficiency, and data inaccuracy.

The strategy I proposed was built around three tools: Google Workspace for centralizing content and automating workflows, Slack for structured volunteer coordination and automated attendance tracking, and Salesforce CRM for real-time impact measurement and student progress analytics.

What I'm most proud of is that I wasn't just recommending off-the-shelf tools — I was connecting each recommendation back to their specific operational pain points and showing how it would help them scale sustainably. It was a great experience because it pushed me to apply PM and consulting thinking in a completely different context — resource-constrained, mission-driven, no existing tech infrastructure."

---

## 21. What I Don't Know Yet: Questions for Satvik

**Question 1: Amex Outcome Metrics.** The previously documented metrics (SUS, Task Completion Rate, Time-to-Investigation, Score Interpretation Errors, data quality 8%) have all been confirmed as incorrect. The only confirmed metric is ~70% reduction in speed to market (10 days → 3 days). Are there any additional accurate, verifiable metrics from the CRR work or the CDL strategy work that can be used on a resume? For CDL, potential metrics include the data compression ratios (136K→64 categories for Product Type), the 54 data points scoped, the 1.7%→58%→96.5% coverage progression, or the number of risk elements impacted.

**Question 2: Attentive.ai Stories.** Are there any experiences from your career that aren't on the current resumes but are particularly relevant to Attentive.ai? For example, domain-heavy user personas (like compliance analysts at Amex who resisted change), complex workflows you simplified, or TAT-focused product work. You mentioned you're not certain but there can be -- to revisit later.

**Question 3: Negative News AI Agent Status.** The Work Fusion EvantAgent integration for negative news and adverse media triage -- what is the current status? Is it deployed or still being built? What are the measurable outcomes so far?

**Question 4: CDL Metrics and Outcomes.** The CDL strategy has been built and execution is underway from PI 26.1. What measurable outcomes have been achieved so far? Has the Phase 0 (pre-standardization discovery) been completed? Are there any early wins or metrics from the execution?

**Question 5: Advocacy Adoption.** Do you know if any of your Advocacy strategy recommendations at Sprinklr were adopted after you left?

**Question 6: LinkRight Suite Completeness.** LinkRight has 4-5 products inside it (Sync, Flex, Scout, Shipquick, etc.). What is the current status of each? Which ones are functional and which are still in development?
